coredata.dc:title: Striking antithermal quenching and waterproofness of Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors induced by synergy of double coating and single passivation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuelan
Ruan, Henghuan
Lei, Jun
Wang, Tianman
Wang, Lin
Gao, Weiyue
Liao, Sen
Huang, Yingheng
coredata.dc:description: Manganese (IV) (Mn4+)-doped fluoride phosphors generally suffer two pressing problems, thermal quenching (TQ) and poor water resistance. This study reports striking luminous intensity enhancement, anti-TQ property, and waterproofness of Mn4+-doped potassium fluosilicate phosphors induced by synergy of double coating and single passivation (coating with hydroxylated graphene quantum dots (GQDs), passivation with H2O2, and coating with K2SiF6). The luminous intensity of the phosphor is approximately 163 % of that of K2SiF6:Mn4+. The integrated emission intensity at 150 °C is 129.3 % higher than that at 30 °C. The emission intensity remains 93.2 % of the initial value after immersion in water for 480 min. These results are attributed to energy transfer from GQDs to Mn4+ and the formation of a stable Mn-free shell layer. In particular, the white light-emitting diodes produced using the phosphors maintained 99.6 % luminescence efficiency after 240 h of operation in high-temperature, high-humidity environments. We believe that this study provides a feasible approach to simultaneously enhance the thermal stability and waterproofness of Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mn4+
Antithermal quenching
Waterproof
Energy transfer
White light-emitting diode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of KSFM, KSFMG, KSFMG-P, KSFMG-E and KSFMG-PE; (b) Mn 2p XPS high-resolution spectra of KSFM and KSFMG. (c) Mn 2p XPS high-resolution spectrum of KSFMG-P, KSFMG-E, KSFMG-PE; (d) FT-IR spectra of KSFM, GQDs, KSFMG and KSFMG-PE.
SEM images of (a) KSFM, (b) KSFMG, (c) KSFMG-P, (d) KSFMG-E, (e) KSFMG-PE; (f) EDS mapping images of KSFMG-PE; HRTEM images of (g, j) KSFM、(h, k) KSFMG、(i, l) KSFMG-PE; (m) HRTEM image of KSFMG.

PL spectra of (a) KSF:x mol% Mn4+ and (b) KSF:0.06Mn4+@y mg/mol GQDs; (c) PL spectra of KSFM, KSFMG, and OH-GQDs at room temperature; (d) luminescence efficiency, and (e) emission decay curves of KSFMG containing different dosages of GQDs; (f) Diagram of energy level transition induced by GQDs.
Tanabe-Sugano energy-level diagram of Mn4+ ions in octahedral crystal field.
(a) Excitation and emission spectra and (b) luminescence efficiency of KSFMG, KSFMG-P, KSFMG-E, and KSFMG-PE.

Photographs of KSFM, KSFMG-P, KSFMG-E, and KSFMG-PE (a) after immersion in water for different times and (b) relative emission intensity change (λex = 467 nm) after immersion in water for different times.
Emission spectra of KSFM, KSFMG-P, KSFMG-E and KSFMG-PE immersed in water for 480 min. The inset shows the relative intensity changes.

(a–c) Emission spectra of KSFM, KSFMG, and KSFMG-PE at room temperature to 493 K and corresponding (d) temperature-dependent normalized integrated intensity curves; (e–g) Emission spectra and relative emission intensities of KSFMG-PE at 467 nm excitation with continuous monitoring at 373 K, 433 K, and 493 K for 75 min at each temperature for 25 min with a time interval of 5 min.
Emission spectra and parameter indices of (a) WLED-1 and WLED-2; (b) CIE chromaticity coordinate changes and (c) electroluminescence (EL) changes of WLED-1 and WLED-2 after different times.

Spectroscopic parameters of Mn4+ in different fluoride crystals.

is crystal field strength, B and C are the Racah parameters. E

level.

head.ce:title: Striking antithermal quenching and waterproofness of Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors induced by synergy of double coating and single passivation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, Guangxi, 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Manganese (IV) (Mn4+)-doped fluoride phosphors generally suffer two pressing problems, thermal quenching (TQ) and poor water resistance. This study reports striking luminous intensity enhancement, anti-TQ property, and waterproofness of Mn4+-doped potassium fluosilicate phosphors induced by synergy of double coating and single passivation (coating with hydroxylated graphene quantum dots (GQDs), passivation with H2O2, and coating with K2SiF6). The luminous intensity of the phosphor is approximately 163 % of that of K2SiF6:Mn4+. The integrated emission intensity at 150 °C is 129.3 % higher than that at 30 °C. The emission intensity remains 93.2 % of the initial value after immersion in water for 480 min. These results are attributed to energy transfer from GQDs to Mn4+ and the formation of a stable Mn-free shell layer. In particular, the white light-emitting diodes produced using the phosphors maintained 99.6 % luminescence efficiency after 240 h of operation in high-temperature, high-humidity environments. We believe that this study provides a feasible approach to simultaneously enhance the thermal stability and waterproofness of Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors.

K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs-surface passivation combined with ion exchange (KSFMG-PE) induced by the synergistic action of triple treatment (hydroxylated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coating, passivation with H2O2, and coating with K2SiF6) on K2SiF6:Mn4+ (KSFM) surface for luminescence intensity enhancement, antithermal quenching, and waterproofness. The white light emitting diodes assembled on the basis of KSFMG-PE can maintain 99.6 % luminous efficiency after 240 h of operation in high-temperature, high-humidity environments.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: surface passivation combined with ion exchange

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) converted from phosphors have emerged as an essential lighting source owing to their high efficiency, long lifetime, low energy consumption, and environmental friendliness [1–3]. Typical WLED devices can be fabricated using two combinatorial strategies: (a) a blue light-emitting diode (LED) chip and a yellow phosphor, and (b) a near-ultraviolet (n-UV) LED chip and a tricolor phosphor [4]. Irrespective of the fabrication strategy, use of red phosphors is essential for enhancing the illumination quality and tuning the correlated color temperature of WLEDs. Manganese (IV) (Mn4+) is a potential red-emission activator ion for red phosphors [5–9]. Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphor has successfully exhibited narrowband red-light emission (emission peak located at 600–700 nm) and high luminescence efficiency (LE) (QY > 98 %). Therefore, considerable research has been conducted on Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors regarding their controlled synthesis, photoluminescence (PL) properties, theoretical simulations, and device packaging, laying the foundation for their development and adoption [4,10–12]. However, toxic hydrofluoric acid and its extremely high chemical and environmental stabilities have restricted the commercial application of WLEDs.

Typically, the heat generated during WLED operations (≥423 K) is prone to severe emission losses due to the nonradiative transition of the electrons from the excited to ground state. This is called thermal quenching (TQ) [13], a notable obstacle to practical application of phosphors in WLEDs. Several efforts have been made to design various methods to tackle the heat-emission loss at elevated temperatures. Recently, Dang et al. systematically discussed the mechanism of TQ and reviewed several important design strategies for TQ-resistant phosphors, including defect engineering, energy transfer, structural modulation, increased crystallinity, layered design structures, negative/zero thermal expansion, surface coating, and glass techniques [14]. These design methods assist in solving the TQ problem of many fluorescent materials.

The transition metal ion Mn4+ exhibits a 3d–3d parity forbidden transition, largely subject to the crystal field. Senden et al. reported that quenching of Mn4+-doped fluorides proceeds through a thermally activated crossover between the 4T2 excited state and 4A2 ground state and that the quenching temperature can be optimized by designing a host lattice in which Mn4+ has high 4T2 excited-state energy [12]. The TQ-resistant red phosphor Mg3Y2Ge3O12:Eu3+,Mn4+, with continuous emission enhancement from 298 to 523 K, was developed by devising energy transfer from Eu3+ to Mn4+ [15]. Mg3Y2Ge3O12:Eu3+,Mn4+ phosphor is able to rapidly realize the anti-TQ due to the enhancement of effective energy transfer, energy compensation and structural stiffness [9,15–17].

In Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors, the inherent instability and susceptibility to hydrolysis of Mn4+ is the other concerning limitation with respect to their adoption in WLED lighting [18,19]. Formation of organic or inorganic shell layers on the surface of Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors is a viable approach to improve their water stability [20–24]. By constructing K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6 using the reverse cation exchange strategy, the outer K2TiF6 layer can not only block the contact between water and Mn4+ but also effectively cut off energy transfer to the surface defects [20]. However, the outer layer still contains a low concentration of Mn4+ owing to the reversible nature of the reaction. Post-treatment with reducing agents can effectively eliminate Mn4+ from the surface. However, the fluoride shell layer formed by the reaction must be sufficiently thick to effectively resist the moisture resistance [21,22]. In the experiments in this paper, when K2SiF6:Mn4+ (KSFM) reached a dissolution–crystallization equilibrium in an HF–K2SiF6 solution, the Mn4+ in the solution was removed by H2O2 to prevent Mn4+ from newly entering the crystal surface, creating a true Mn-free shell layer that can withstand extreme-humidity conditions.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs), both being a new addition to the family of carbon materials, are typically classified as carbon nanoparticles with a size of <10 nm. Li et al. fabricated NaYF4:Er,Yb (Tm)/carbon dot composites, which are bimodal luminescent materials, using a direct solvothermal method [25]. Zhang et al. synthesized Carbon dots (CD) -modified P25/TiO2 composites via a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction. In these composites, CDs serve as an electron reservoir to promote the effective splitting of photoinduced electron–hole pairs of P25 [26]. The considerably increased photocatalytic activity implies effective energy transfer from the CDs to P25. This approach motivated us to prepare the corresponding composites using K2SiF6 and GQDs as precursors. We synthesized KSFM@GQDs-PE (KSFMG-PE, PE is surface passivation combined with ion exchange), a red phosphor with anti-TQ and weatherproofing properties (Fig. 1 ), by synergy of double coating and single passivation (coated GQDs, H2O2 passivation, and coated substrate). Energy transfer occurs from the GQDs to Mn4+, and the K2SiF6 shell layer blocks energy transfer to the surface, enhancing the luminescence properties and thermal stability of the phosphor. The structure, luminescence properties, anti-TQ mechanism, and application of the phosphor were comprehensively investigated. The synthesized KSFMG-PE phosphor exhibits an internal quantum efficiency (IQE) of 99.98 %, a lifetime of 8.17 ms, remarkable anti-TQ performance (129.4 %@423 K), and excellent water resistance (93 %@480 min). Moreover, it is superior to commercial KSFM (IQE of 84.8 %, lifetime of 8.06 ms). Furthermore, the performance of WLEDs assembled using KSFMG-PE as the red component was investigated over time in high-temperature (85 °C) and high-humidity (85 %) environments.
The experimental contents and characterization are described in the Supplementary Materials (Figs. S1–S6 and Tables S1–S3). Fig. 1 is a schematic diagram of the synthesis route of KSFMG-PE.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed to obtain structural information of the samples. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the peaks observed at 18.9°, 31.1°, 38.3°, and 44.5° correspond to the (111), (220), (222), and (400) planes of K2SiF6 (JCPDS 75–0694), respectively. The synthesized samples are all pure phase K2SiF6.

X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to investigate the chemical state of the surface of the KSFM and KSFMG. Fig. S1 shows that KSFMG comprises potassium (K), silicon (Si), fluorine (F), Mn, and carbon (C). Two distinct Mn signal lines, Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, were observed in the high-resolution XPS spectra of the KSFMG sample. Mn 2p produces four peaks (Fig. 2b): the first double peaks located at 641.77 and 653.59 eV are attributed to the Mn4+ state, and the second double peaks at 646.33 and 657.28 eV are assigned to the Mn3+ state [27]. Notably, the characteristic peak corresponding to the Mn 2p3/2 orbital of KSFMG is shifted by 0.3 eV toward the low binding energy relative to KSFM, indicating that the Mn4+ in KSFMG probably gains some electrons. Similarly, the Mn 2p XPS test spectra of KSFM@GQDs-surface passivation (KSFMG-P), K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs-ion exchange (KSFMG-E), and K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQD-surface passivation combined with ion exchange (KSFMG-PE) are shown in Fig. 2c.

The Mn conent in KSFMG-P and KSFMG-E is remarkably diminished, indicating that both methods remove the surface Mn atoms effectively but not completely. The Mn 2p characteristic peak of KSFMG-PE appears flatter, and the surface Mn content is substantially lower than that in the samples treated using other methods, indicating the formation of a true Mn-free shell layer. The Fourier transform infrared spectra of KSFM, GQDs, KSFMG, and KSFMG-PE were recorded and compared to further confirm the composition of KSFMG and KSFMG-PE.

The stretching vibration of F–Si–F at ∼483 cm−1 was detected in case of KSFM, KSFMG, and KSFMG-PE, as shown in Fig. 2d [28]. The characteristic vibrational absorption peaks corresponding to GQDs were detected in case of KSFMG and KSFMG-PE. The band observed at 2883–2951 cm−1 was attributed to the infrared absorption of the C–H bond. The absorption peak observed at ∼1651 cm−1 corresponded to the infrared absorption caused by the CC bond stretching vibration. The infrared absorption peak observed at ∼1455 cm−1 corresponded to the C–H stretching vibration. The infrared absorption band observed at 1041–1085 cm−1 corresponded to the C–O stretching vibration [29].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the KSFM and KSFMG phosphors exhibited smooth surfaces (Fig. 3 a and b), illustrating that the hydrothermal treatment does not alter their surface morphology. The KSFMG-P surface treated with H2O2 remained smooth but appeared to have a structure similar to that of a possible K2SiF6 layer (Fig. 3c). In comparison, the surface of the KSFMG-E and KSFMG-PE samples exhibited many small particles and pits of different depths due to the corrosive effect of high HF concentration on the surface lattice (Fig. 3d and e). The Mn4+ contents of KSFM, KSFMG, KSFMG-P, KSFMG-E, and KSFMG-PE, obtained via energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), were 0.516 wt%, 0.396 wt%, 0.430 wt%, 0.695 wt %, and 0.030 wt%, respectively (Table S4), indicating that the Mn content of KSFMG-PE is considerably lesser than that of the other samples. The EDS mapping results of KSFMG-PE showed that K, Si, F, and C were uniformly distributed on the phosphor, but Mn atoms were found to be relatively less, microscopically indicating that a KSF shell layer might have been formed (Fig. 3f). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 3g–i) revealed that the particle size of the phosphor was 10–20 μm, which is consistent with the SEM images.

No shell layer structure was observed on the surface of KSFM (Fig. 3j). Some granular material was adhered to the surface of KSFMG (see Fig. 3k), while a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (the high-resolution TEM image shown in Fig. 3m) confirmed that the granular material was GQDs [29]. Fig. 3l shows that the thickness of the surface passivation layer on the KSFMG-PE sample was ∼80 nm. All these results indicated successful preparation of KSFMG-PE.

The PL effect was optimized using the controlled variable method for the reaction temperature, reaction time, and GQDs dosage. First, the reaction time and GQD dosage were set to 3 h of hydrothermal treatment time and 10 mg/mol, respectively, and the reaction temperature was increased from 353 to 433 K. Fig. S2a shows the XRD patterns of KSFMG at various reaction temperatures. No excess spurious peaks are observed, and the diffraction peaks of all samples correspond to those of the standard card JCPDS 75–0694, demonstrating that all samples synthesized are of pure phases. This result illustrates that increasing the reaction temperature does not alter the phase purity of the KSFMG phosphor. The maximum value of the emission spectra of KSFMG at 413 K decreases as the reaction temperature increases to 433 K. Phosphors prepared at various reaction temperatures are shown in Fig. S2b. The photoluminescence increases as the reaction temperature increases from 353 K to 413 K, reaching a maximum, and then decreases as the reaction temperature increases to 433 K.

This is because as the reaction temperature increases, more GQDs are deposited on the KSM surface and the effect of enhanced luminescence becomes more obvious. However, the luminescence intensity gradually decreases when the temperature increases to 433 K, probably because Mn4+ is unstable at high temperatures [30]. Hence, this reaction temperature experiment suggests 413 K as the optimal synthesis temperature for KSFMG.

The synthesis temperature was set to 413 K, while the dosage of GQDs was set to 10 mg/mol. Further, the reaction time adjusted from 2 to 6 h with 1-h intervals. Fig. S2c shows the XRD patterns of KSFMG at different reaction times. The emission spectra are shown in Fig. S2d, which shows that KSFMG exhibited the strongest photoluminescence at a hydrothermal time of 3 h. The PL intensity decreased with increasing reaction time (4–6 h) owing to the instability of Mn4+ in the solution over an extended time.

The dosages of GQDs were optimized by fixing the synthesis temperature at 413 K and the hydrothermal time at 3 h. Fig. S3 shows the XRD patterns of KSFMG at various GQDs dosages. Fig. 4 shows the relationship between the doping concentration of Mn4+ and GQDs and the corresponding luminescence properties of the samples. Fig. 4a shows that all KSF:x mol% Mn4+ (x = 1, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15 mol%) samples have similar PL spectra but with different intensities. The inset of Fig. 4a shows that the emission intensity is considerably affected by the Mn4+ concentration. The intensity reaches its maximum at x = 6 %. The PL spectra of KSFM@y mg/mol GQDs (y = 0, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 mg/mol) are shown in Fig. 4b. The luminescence intensity of Mn4+ increased to 163 % when the amount of GQDs reached 8 mg/mol. Fig. 4c shows that the emission peak of GQDs under 467-nm excitation is located at 513 nm. However, KSFMG has no emission peak at the same location. Although the emission spectra of the GQDs do not coincide with the excitation spectra of the phosphors, the phenomenon of phosphor luminescence is still enhanced possibly due to the enhanced Förster resonance energy transfer from the GQDs to Mn4+ ions. The above phenomenon and conclusion were confirmed by other researchers as well [31–33]. Zhang et al. reported enhanced luminescence intensity of Ln3+ (Eu3+) ions using CQDs and attributed it to the enhanced energy transfer from CQDs to Ln3+ ions [31]. Samples of the KSFM@y mg/mol GQDs (y = 0, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 mg/mol) series were characterized to further illustrate the influence of GQDs on the quantum efficiency (QE) of Mn4+-doped fluorinated phosphors. The absorption efficiency (AE), IQE, and external quantum efficiency were calculated [22] as in Eqs. (1)–(3):
(1)
AE =
∫ λ
[ E
( λ )
− R
( λ )
]
d λ
∫ λ E
( E )
d λ
(2)
IQE =
∫ λ P
( λ )
d λ
∫ λ E
( λ )
d λ
(3)
EQE = IQE × AE =
∫ λ P
( λ )
d λ
∫ λ E
( λ )
d λ
where
E
( λ )
/ h v
,
R
( λ )
/ h v
, and
R
( λ )
/ h v
are the number of photons in the phosphor excitation spectrum, reflection spectrum, and emission spectrum, respectively. The IQE of these samples was tested under 467 nm excitation light, as shown in Fig. 4d and S4a–b. The IQE of KSFM@y mg/mol GQDs phosphors first increasing and then decreasing with the increase of GQDs dosage, and the maximum value is 96.5 %. The decay times of KSFM@y mg/mol GQDs (y = 0, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 mg/mol) monitoring the 630 nm emission band were also investigated in support of this spectral analysis, as shown in Fig. 4e. The data allows for fitting with a single exponential decay model as shown in Eq. (4) [34]:
(4)
I
( t )
= A × exp
(
− t
τ
)
+
I 0
where I(t) represents the luminous intensity at a specific time t, A represents a constant, and τ represents luminous lifetime. The decay lifetimes were computed as 8.05, 8.09, 8.12, 8.17, 8.14, and 8.13 ms using Eq. (5), corresponding to x = 0, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 mg/mol, respectively. GQDs can inhibit the nonradiative transition of Mn4+, but excess GQDs can result in a concentration burst effect. Similar proofs of the effect were found in other previously conducted studies, such as studies on GdVO4:Eu3+/CDs [35] and His-GQD-HDA-NaYF4:Yb, Er [36].

GQDs contain hydroxyl functional groups (–OH) on their surface. Transition metal ions interact with the –OH groups to form metal–GQDs complexes [37–39]. Therefore, the coated GQDs, supported by the interaction between them and Mn4+, not only enhanced the absorption of excitation light by the sample but also decreased the nonradiative transition probability (from 2Eg to 4A2g) and increased the radiative transition probability (from 2Eg to 4A2g). The mechanism of fluorescence enhancement due to coated GQDs is shown in Fig. 4f and supported by the results in Fig. 4a–d.

The effect of the octahedral environment on the energy levels of Mn4+ can be illustrated by the Tanabe-Sugano diagram [40], as shown in Fig. 5 . Based on the PLE spectrum of K2SiF6:Mn4+, the energy gaps for the
A
2 g
4
→
T
1 g
4
and
A
2 g
4
→
T
2 g
4
transitions of Mn4+ are calculated to be 27778 cm−1 and 21413 cm−1, respectively. In the K2SiF6 matrix, the crystal field strength
D q
of Mn4+ can be determined by Eq. (5) [41]:
(5)

(6)

According to the PL spectrum of K2SiF6:Mn4+, the peak energy of

of Mn4+ is 15873 cm−1, and the Racah parameter C is determined using Eq. (8) [41]:

D q
The B and C values of K2SiF6:Mn4+ are 593 cm−1 and 3765 cm−1, respectively, and thus
D q
/B is equal to 3.611, indicating that Mn4+ has a strong crystal field in the K2SiF6 matrix. Table 1 collects some of the crystal field strength
D q
and Racah parameters of Mn4+ in different crystals. The table shows that the variation of
E
2
g
energy level of fluoride is small. Among them, the
E
2
g
of K2SiF6:Mn4+ by different synthesis methods ranges from 15873 cm−1 to 16100 cm−1 with an average value of 15951.8 cm−1, which is comparable to that of K2SiF6:Mn4+ synthesized here with
E
2
g
energy level is close to the energy of the synthesized K2SiF6:Mn4+ in this paper.

The KSFMG, KSFMG-P, KSFMG-E, and KSFMG-PE samples exhibited almost identical excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 6 a). Fig. 6b shows the QEs of the four fluoride phosphors. The IQE of KSFMG is 96.51 %, while that of the phosphors treated with P (surface passivation), E (cation exchange), and PE (surface passivation combined with ion exchange) reaches 95.64 %, 95.93 %, and 99.98 %, respectively. The P and E treatments do not decrease the IQE, while the PE treatment increases the IQE of the phosphor. The energy migration probability and migration distance of Mn4+ will increase with increasing Mn4+ concentration [51,52], and the reduction of Mn4+ on the phosphor surface can cut off the migration path of energy to the surface defects, thus increasing the IQE. The AE values reveal the changes in Mn4+ distribution on the phosphor surface after the different treatments. The EQE values of KSFMG, KSFMG-P, and KSFMG-E are similar at 72.8, 72.8, and 72.9 %, respectively, while the AE value of KSFMG-PE is 73.6 % (Fig. S4c), which is inferior to that of KSFMG, indicating that a large portion of the Mn4+ has been eliminated from the surface.

The fluorescence thermal stability of phosphors that emit at high temperatures is commonly considered a fundamental criterion for assessing their application potential [53]. The emission spectra under 467-nm excitation of KSFM, KSFMG, and KSFMG-PE at temperatures ranging from 298 K to 493 K are shown in Fig. 9 a–c to elucidate the effect of GQDs and PE treatment methods on the fluorescence thermal stability of KSFM. The spectra of Mn4+ did not shift with increasing temperature.

The temperature-dependent normalized integrated intensity curves of KSFM, KSFMG, and KSFMG-PE are shown in Fig. 9d. All three samples follow an increasing and subsequently decreasing trend. KSFM attains its maximum value at 333 K and then exhibits a falling trend, while KSFMG and KSFMG-PE reach their maximum value at 393 K and assume a similar curve shape.

The detailed results presented in Fig. 9d for the KSFM, KSFMG, and KSFMG-PE are as follows: (a) at 393 K (120 °C), the integrated emission intensities of the samples are 93.8 %, 125.3 %, and 130.0 % of their initial values, respectively; and (b) at 423 K (150 °C), their integrated emission intensities are 82.2 %, 121.3 %, and 129.4 % of their initial values, respectively. Therefore, it can be determined that the GQDs contribute the most to the thermal burst resistance, followed by the contribution of the KSF shells obtained using the PE method. In practical applications, WLEDs are required to endure high-temperature and high-humidity operating environments. The humidity resistance and anti-TQ properties of typical Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors are listed in Table S1. Compared with the phosphors presented in Table S1, KSFMG-PE phosphors have more remarkable anti-TQ and waterproof performance.

The PL spectra detected at 373, 433, and 493 K were recorded to further investigate the application potential of KSFMG-PE, as shown in Fig. 9e–g. The emission intensity and peak wavelength were held constant for 25 min at each temperature. Results show that KSFMG-PE has superior fluorescence thermal stability and is appropriate for high-power WLED applications.

I
s and I
a denote the Stokes and anti-Stokes emission intensities, respectively, when I
ZPL is 0 (ZPL refers to zero-phonon line). Considering the acoustic phonon contribution αT and assuming E ZPL ± hv j = E ZPL, I
s and I
a can be described as in Eqs. (9) and (10) [54]
(9)
I s
( T )
= c
∑ J
|
(
i |
M j
)
f
| 2
(
E
Z P L
)
4
(
1 +
n j
+ α T
)
(10)
I a
( T )
= c
∑ J
|
(
i |
M j
)
f
| 2
(
E
Z P L
)
4
(
n j
+ α T
)
where c represents a scaling constant, |(i|M
j |f)| represents a light transition operator (i = initial state; f = final state; M
j = momentum matrix element), E
ZPL represents the ZPL emission energy, h represents Planck constant, ν
j represents the appropriate weighted-average frequency of the various lattice vibrations (j = vibration mode), T represents temperature, and n
j represents the Bose–Einstein occupation number. We observed that the I
s and I
a of KSFMG continuously increase until 453 K and 473 K, respectively, and that the increase in I
a values exceeds that in I
s . The I
s and I
a of KSFM were maximum at 333 and 393 K, respectively, and then declined at respective higher temperatures. The high temperature simultaneously accelerated the energy loss of the anti-Stokes and Stokes emissions and reduced the intensity of the emission peak via nonradiative transitions.

Furthermore, owing to the energy compensation from the GQDs, the number of occupied phonons in the KSFMG lattice increased with increasing temperature, and the electron–phonon interaction was enhanced, resulting in gradual increase in the sidebands of the anti-Stokes peak that corresponded to the phonon absorption process [54,55]. Thus, anti-TQ can be summarized as an increase in phonon occupancy, aided via enhancement in the induced 2Eg→4A2g emission, which prohibits parity check and spin [56]. The decay lifetimes of Mn4+ emission in KSFM and KSFMG were measured at different temperatures, as shown in Fig. 10b and c. The fluorescence lifetime of Mn4+ shortens at elevated temperatures, unlike KSFMG, for which the lifetime decay is lower than KSFM's (Fig. 10d). The results indicate that extra energy is transferred from GQDs to Mn4+. In turn, KSFMG-PE shows a marginally stronger anti-TQ effect than that shown by KSFMG primarily because the KSF shell layer prevents the oxidation of the internal [MnF6]2- group [57]. The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates and color purity of the KSFMG-PE phosphors were calculated at different temperatures (Fig. S5 and Table S2). The color stability and slight drop in color purity indicate that KSFMG-PE has favorable thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and performance of WLED devices
ce:section.ce:para: The color stability of WLED devices is dependent on the environmental stability of the phosphor used. WLED-1 and WLED-2 were fabricated using KSFM and KSFMG-PE packaged with blue chips (∼450 nm) and commercial YAG:Ce3+, respectively (weight ratio of YAG:Ce3+ to red phosphor was 1:3), to assess the stability of KSFMG-PE in LED devices. Changes in luminescence performance and long-term stability were also examined during exposure to high-temperature (85 °C), high-humidity (85 %) environments for 240 h.

The LE of WLED-1 and WLED-2 were 103.8 lm/W (correlated color temperature (CCT) = 3386 K; Ra = 94.6) and 104.1 lm/W (CCT = 3115 K; Ra = 96.0) at a driving current of 20 mA, respectively (Fig. 11 a). The chromaticity coordinates also denote the long-term stability of the WLED devices. The CIE chromaticity coordinates are plotted in Fig. 11b. The CIE color coordinates of WLED-1 and WLED-2 are (x = 0.4025, y = 0.3690) and (x = 0.4358, y = 0.4158), respectively, and they are both positioned immediately adjacent to the blackbody radiation curve in the CIE 1931 color space, meaning that the packaged white LEDs comply with warm-white light standards (0.4476, 0.4074). After 240 h of operation, the x and y coordinates of WLED-1 are shifted by 0.0153 and 0.0123, respectively. In contrast, the x and y coordinates of WLED-2 shifted by 0.0014 and 0.0010, respectively (see Fig. 11b and Table S3), and the color shift of the latter was smaller than that of the former. More importantly, the LE decay of WLED-2 is also considerably lower than that of WLED-1 (Fig. 11c), with 98.2 and 103.7 lm/W for WLED-1 and WLED-2, respectively, after 240 h of operation.

Herein, the KSFMG-PE red-emission phosphor obtained under the synergistic action of double coating and single passivation (coating with GQDs, passivation with H2O2, and coating with K2SiF6) achieved remarkable improvement in the three luminescence properties at the same time. The detailed results are as follows: (a) The luminous intensity was approximately 163 % of that of KSFM. (b) The integrated emission intensity at 423 K was 129.3 % higher than that at 298K. (c) The emission intensity remained 93.2 % of the initial value after 480-min immersion in water. Finally, an LE of 104.1 lm/W at 20 mA driving current was obtained using the KSFMG-PE phosphor, which maintained an initial LE of 99.6 % after 240 h of exposure to high-temperature, high-humidity environments. We believe that this strategy paves a novel way to enhance the luminescence properties and thermal stability of Mn4+-doped fluoride phosphors.

Yuelan Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Henghuan Ruan: Investigation. Jun Lei: Investigation. Tianman Wang: Investigation. Lin Wang: Investigation. Weiyue Gao: Investigation. Sen Liao: Supervision, Conceptualization. Yingheng Huang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21965004), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, China (Grant No. 2019GXNSFDA245022, No. 2020GXNSFAA159036), Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. YCBZ2023006), and the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (Grant No. XDZ140116).
